notes: The name of a great bodhisattva who represents great compassion.
The Buddha; the Lord.
A venerable teacher; an epithet of a buddha.
First of the gods of the Brahma class.
The heaven in which the Brahma gods live.
Supreme class of gods.
The last of the three ages of the Dharma, following the age of the Semblance Dharma, in which only the teaching of the Buddha exists but correct practice is no longer possible.
A cousin of the Buddha who became his disciple but later tried to murder him and assume leadership of the sangha.
The manifestation of the Buddha as ultimate reality.
A work corresponding to the Pali Dhammapada.
A derogatory term applied by Mahayanists to the various schools of Buddhism that exalt as their ideal the arhat, the religious adept who has transcended all passions.
A mythological continent south of Mount Sumeru, q.v.
Great China; China.
A form of Buddhism that developed in India around 100 B.C.E. and exalts as its religious ideal the bodhisattva.
A certain god; often Siva.
Great Lotus Hell.
The bodhisattva who represents great wisdom.
The Evil One; the Devil.
Homage (to).
Visnu.
A Jain.
Wicked (epithet of Mara).
The demon that causes eclipses.
Indra; the lord of the gods (so-called, but below Brahma).
Sakra, the lord of the gods (of a certain level).
The historical Buddha, who lived in India in the fifth century B.C.E. and whose life and teachings form the basis for Buddhism.
The name of the historical Buddha Sakyamuni's family clan.
One who is completely awake; a Buddha.
One who is truly and completely awakened; a Buddha.
A certain school of Buddhism.
The second of the three ages of the Buddhist Dharma, following the age of the True Dharma, in which the Buddha's teaching is practiced but enlightenment is no longer possible.
Literally, "he who has attained happiness" (or "he who has gone well"); a designation for a buddha.
A mythological mountain, the axis of the world.
Literally, "he who has thus gone (or come)"; a designation for a buddha.
The Buddha, the Dharma (Doctrine), and the Sangha (Order).
Heaven of Sakra.
Three Baskets, an early collection of Buddhist texts.
The first of the three ages of the Buddhist Dharma, in which the Buddhist teaching is properly practiced and enlightenment can be attained.
A Buddhist school, founded in the fourth century C.E., which advocated the doctrine of "mind only" or "conscious construction only."
Teacher.
Early Buddhist texts, most of which are extant only in Chinese translation; they correspond to the Pali nikayas.
The stage of samadhi (q.v.) in which nothing exists; the third stage of the four stages of the formless world.
Non-returner; one who need not be reborn on this earth; one who is in the third of the four stages of development.
Complete, perfect enlightenment.
A class of demonic beings.
Wilderness or forest, regarded as the ideal place to engage in the practices of a renunciant.
Pertaining to the forest; a forest-dweller; a forested place.
A forest-dwelling monk.
Dwelling in the forest.
"Incalculable"; a very high number.
A kind of flower.
Story, parable; one of the twelve types of scriptures.
The Good Kalpa, in which a thousand Buddhas will appear.
Path of development, cultivation, or meditation.
A Buddhist monk.
A Buddhist nun.
Samsara; the round of rebirth.
Lit., the mind (citta) of awakening or enlightenment (bodhi). The aspiration for enlightenment.
The priestly caste in the Indian caste system; in the Lotus Sutra the term also applies to a class of heavenly beings.
A cosmic world or realm in which a particular buddha dwells.
The state of becoming or being a buddha; the goal of the bodhisattva path.
An outcaste; a violent person, a butcher.
Moon, splendor.
Patron, almsgiver, donor.
The doctrine that all things come into being only in dependence upon other things.

The Buddhist doctrine which holds that all phenomena (dharmas) arise in relation to causes and conditions and in turn are the causes and conditions for the arising of other phenomena. Nothing exists independently of its causes and conditions.
A class of supernatural beings; a god or divine being.
Mnemonic device or formula; also, spell or incantation.
An ascetic practice, such as dwelling in the forest.
Ascetic practices, of which generally twelve are listed in the Mahayana.
Meditation or contemplation.

Eight conditions under which it is difficult to encounter a Buddha: (1) in the hells, (2) as hungry ghosts, (3) as animals, (4) in Uttarakuru (the northern continent where all is pleasant), (5) in the long-life heavens (where life is long and easy), (6) as deaf, blind, and dumb, (7) as a worldly philosopher, (8) in the intermediate period between a Buddha and his successor.

Eight places or circumstances where one is unable to see the Buddha or listen to the Dharma: (1) hell, (2) the realm of hungry ghosts, (3) the realm of animals, (4) the heavens of long life, (5) the border regions, (6) the state of having impaired sense organs, (7) the state of having erroneous views, and (8) the land where a tathagata has not appeared.
Usually, (1) lions, (2) elephants, (3) fire, (4) snakes, (5) thieves, (6) violent waters, (7) imprisonment, and (8) demons.

The eight births that prevent one from seeing a Buddha or hearing the Dharma are (1) birth in hell; (2) birth in the realm of hungry spirits; (3) birth in the realm of beasts; (4) birth in the heaven of long life, where aspiration to enter the Buddhist Path does not arise; (5) birth in a remote land, in one of the heavens of the realms of form and formlessness or in Uttarakuru, one of the four continents in Buddhist cosmology, where sentient beings are absorbed in pleasures and do not seek the Dharma; (6) being born blind, deaf, or mute; (7) being knowledgeable about the world and eloquent; and (8) being born in a period before or after the Buddha's appearance in the world.
See eight great fears.

(1) Not to kill, (2) not to steal, (3) not to engage in sexual acts, (4) not to lie, (5) not to drink liquor, (6) not to use high or broad beds, (7) not to put perfumes or oils on the body nor to sing or dance, and (8) to eat only at designated times.

(1) birth, (2) old age, (3) sickness, (4) death, (5) the suffering of being separated from those whom one loves, (6) the suffering of associating with those whom one dislikes, (7) the suffering of not getting what one wants, and (8) the suffering inherent in the five aggregates.
See four noble truths.
An alternative term for the five paths.
Five states of transmigratory existence: (1) hell, (2) hungry ghosts (pretas), (3) animals, (4) humans, and (5) gods (devas).
The five elements of form, feeling, conception, mental process, and consciousness which comprise the personality and give rise to the mistaken view of a permanent, inherent self.

Five supernormal powers: (1) supernormal vision, (2) supernormal hearing, (3) the ability to know others' thoughts, (4) the ability to know former lives, and (5) the ability to perform miracles such as appearing anywhere at will.
Four causes of suffering and obstacles to practice: (1) mental afflictions, (2) the five aggregates, (3) death, and (4) the king of demons (Papiyas) in Paranirmitavasavartin Heaven.
Four categories of Buddhist followers: (1) monks, (2) nuns, (3) laymen, and (4) laywomen.

(1) Life is intrinsically full of suffering; (2) our attachments are the cause of suffering; (3) all suffering can be ended; (4) the way to end suffering is by following the Buddha's eightfold path (i.e., right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration).
According to Buddhism, the four possible ways that a being may be born, i.e., 1) from a womb, 2) from an egg, 3) from moisture, or 4) through metamorphosis or spontaneous generation.
Sotapanna (q.v.), sakrdagamin (q.v.), anagamin (q.v.), arhat (q.v.).
A mythological giant bird.
A scriptural stanza.

A form of Buddhism that appeared in India around 100 B.C.E. and which exalts as its religious ideal the bodhisattva, the great being who thoroughly grasps the doctrine of emptiness and is willing to delay his own enlightenment until he can save all sentient beings. Otherwise known as the Mahayana.
A type of tree; its fruit; a rose apple.
A story of a previous life of the Buddha.
An embryo in a certain stage of development.
A wool garment.
Giver of work, i.e., assigner of duties (in a monastery).
A red or yellow monk's robe.
A kind of acacia tree; its wood.
A class of mythical beings, either half bird or half human and half horse, who make celestial music.
A high number, a crore.
A kind of partridge.
A distance; earshot.
A class of evil beings, sorcerers.
A member of the military or ruling class, the second of the four castes.
A class of demonic beings.
A kind of water lily.
A distance; somewhat more than one-third of a mile.
An aged monk.
A great bodhisattva.
A class of snake-like mythical beings.
An athlete.
A class of human sorcerers.
A ritual hand gesture.
A serpent deity, a dragon.
A large unit of numerical measurement, said to be equal to ten million or one hundred billion.
Liberation from the cycle of birth and death, a state in which all passions are extinguished and the highest wisdom (prajna) attained; bodhi, enlightenment.

One who has reached a stage of spiritual quest that frees one from rebirth in the kamadhatu, the world of desire. Such a person is not yet an arhat, and is bound to be born in the upper samsaric world, i.e., in the rupadhatu and arupyadhatu. Though a non-returner does return to the world, ordinary mortals in the world of desire do not see this return.
A type of Indian fig tree.

The second of the four stages of spiritual attainment in the Hinayana; one who has attained this state is subject to rebirth only once in each of the three realms of the triple world before attaining nirvana.
A red lotus.
Great quinquennial assembly.
See six perfections.
Complete nirvana.
A kind of demon.
Transcendental, liberative wisdom; one of the six perfections.
A white lotus.
A class of demonic beings that cause disease in children.
A kind of demon.
The world of endurance, suffering.
One who will be born only once more; one who is in the second of the four stages of development.
Meditation, concentration, (mental) one-pointedness.
Complete awakening.
The Buddhist order, the monastic community.
The sense perceptions that correspond to the six sense organs (eyes, ears, nose, tongue, body, and mind): visual, auditory, olfactory, gustatory, tactile, and mental perceptions.

Seven kinds of precious metals and gems, variously listed in different sources. Also, seven precious things said to be possessed by a wheel-turning king (cakravartin): (1) a wheel, (2) a precious jewel, (3) a queen, (4) a minister, (5) an elephant, (6) a general, and (7) a horse.

The one buddha vehicle, the Mahayana teaching espoused in the Lotus Sutra that leads to complete enlightenment and attainment of buddhahood, contrasted with the teachings of the two Hinayana vehicles. The single vehicle includes and transcends all three vehicles of the sravaka, pratyekabuddha, and bodhisattva paths.
An alternative term for the six paths.
Six states of transmigratory existence: (1) hell, (2) hungry ghosts (pretas), (3) animals, (4) demigods (asuras), (5) humans, and (6) gods (devas).

(1) The realm of the gods, (2) the realm of humans, (3) the realm of fighting demons, (4) the realm of animals, (5) the realm of hungry ghosts, and (6) hell. All beings transmigrate through these realms in the cycle of birth and death in accordance with their karma.
Six kinds of mindfulness directed at (1) the Buddha, (2) the Dharma, (3) the Sangha, (4) morality, (5) charity, and (6) divinities.
Six supernormal powers consisting of the five supernormal faculties plus the ability to eradicate defilements.
The various methods and means used by buddhas and bodhisattvas to guide and teach sentient beings, adapted to their different capacities.
A solitary Buddha who had no teacher, does not teach, and lacks universal compassion.
An ascetic.
A Buddhist novice monk.
Fruition of a sramana.

Originally, a disciple of the historical Buddha, one who directly heard his teachings. Later used as a general term for followers of early Buddhism, to distinguish them from adherents of Mahayana and Tantric Buddhism.
Stream enterer; one who is in the first of the four stages of development.
The state of a stream enterer.
One who has entered the stream of the undefiled Noble Path and is on the way to enlightenment.
A tope; a structure in which the relics of a buddha are placed.
Nectar, the beverage of the gods.
A member of the serf class, the lowest of the four castes.
A Buddhist scripture, a discourse of the Buddha.
A type of palm tree.

A scheme of thirty-seven practices conducive to enlightenment divided into seven groups: (1) four fields of mindfulness, (2) four right exertions, (3) four bases of supernormal power, (4) five faculties, (5) five powers, (6) seven limbs of enlightenment, and (7) the noble eightfold path.

A reference to three of the six supernormal faculties: (1) the ability to know former lives; (2) supernormal vision, especially insight into the future destiny of all beings; and (3) the ability to eradicate defilements in the present.
See three liberations.
Three of the six states of transmigratory existence in which sentient beings transmigrate in retribution for evil deeds: (1) hell, (2) hungry ghosts, and (3) animals.
Three obstacles to practice caused by (1) karma, (2) mental afflictions, and (3) retribution for past deeds.
Three kinds of meditation regarded as gateways to liberation and known also as the three emptinesses: (1) meditation on emptiness, (2) meditation on signlessness, and (3) meditation on wishlessness.

A threefold categorization of moral behavior: (1) precepts of restraint, pertaining to discipline and deportment; (2) precepts for accumulating wholesome qualities; and (3) precepts for bringing benefit to beings.
The hells, hungry ghosts, animals.
The paths of the sravakas, pratyekabuddhas, and bodhisattvas, respectively.
The three evil realms in which sentient beings transmigrate as retribution for evil deeds, namely, (1) hell, (2) the realm of hungry ghosts, and (3) the realm of animals.
The Buddha, the Dharma (the Buddha's teachings), and the Sangha (the community of his followers).

The three realms of samsaric existence in which living beings transmigrate as a result of their karma: 1) the realm of desire (kamadhatu), i.e., the world of ordinary consciousness accompanied by desires; the realm of form (rupadhatu), in which desires have been eliminated but the physical body remains; and the formless realm (arupyadhatu), in which the physical body no longer exists.

The twelvefold cycle of causes and conditions that make up the human condition, namely: (1) ignorance, (2) volitional activity, (3) consciousness, (4) name and form, (5) the six senses, (6) contact, (7) experience, (8) passion, (9) attachment, (10) existence, (11) rebirth, and (12) decay and death.
Two kinds of obstructions to practice: (1) afflictive hindrances caused by mental afflictions and (2) cognitive hindrances due to fundamental misapprehensions about the nature of reality.
The two Hinayana paths of sravakas and pratyekabuddhas.
The ideal king, as conceived of in Indian philosophy.
A teacher of rules.
A Buddhist layman.
A Buddhist laywoman.
A blue lotus.
Lapis lazuli.
A member of the class of peasants, merchants, and artisans, the third of the four castes.
A reanimated corpse-demon.

An ideal universal monarch whose power derives from a wheel obtained at the time of his enthronement; four kinds of cakravartins are distinguished according to the basic element from which his wheel is made (gold, silver, copper, or iron).
Originally, personal disciples of Sakyamuni who heard his teachings; later generally used to mean so-called Hinayana Buddhists.
A class of demonic beings; human flesh-eaters.
A class of demonic sorcerers.
A league; a unit of distance.
The Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha.
The standard form of worship. Nine prostrations are used for the most solemn occasions.
Meditative concentration.
Three basic mental afflictions: (1) desire or greed, (2) hatred or anger, and (3) ignorance or delusion.

Three spheres of transmigratory existence: (1) the realm of desire, i.e., the world of everyday consciousness accompanied by desires; (2) the realm of form, in which desires have been eliminated but the physical body remains; and (3) the realm of nonform, in which the physical body no longer exists.
A formal ceremony in which the abbot ascends the Dharma seat to deliver a teaching. Held on the 1st, 5th, 10th, 15th, 20th, and 25th of each month, and on special occasions.
BDK convention: World-Honored One (not Blessed One).
The two groups of monastic officers: the administrative officers (知事, chiji) on the east and the senior officers (頭首, chōshu) on the west.
A class of mythical beings, celestial musicians.
The five precepts for Buddhist laypeople: (1) Not to kill, (2) not to steal, (3) not to commit adultery, (4) not to lie, and (5) not to use intoxicants.
The five aggregates that make up sentient beings: (1) form (rupa), (2) sensation (vedana), (3) conception (samjna), (4) volition (samskara), and (5) consciousness (vijnana).
The main hall housing the Buddha images. Site of major ceremonies including Nirvana Assembly and Buddha's Birthday.

english: Sūtra of the Buddha's Bequeathed Teaching
notes: Chanted during the Ullambana merit-connection reading.
The head of the monastery. Also called 'master' (師) or 'mountain master' (山主). In T2025, styled as 'the abbot of this mountain' (住持本山).
The abbot's personal attendant. Burns incense, escorts the abbot, manages tea services, and carries the incense box. Multiple attendants may serve (head attendant, guest-receiving attendant, etc.).
The officer responsible for preparing and managing offerings at ceremonies.
The Buddhist order; the monastic community.
The central hall for meditation, meals, and sleeping. Contains the Holy Monk's shrine, platforms for sitting and sleeping, and the mallet. Also called the 'Cloud Hall' (雲堂, undō).
The bell before the Sangha hall, struck to signal the abbot's entry and other transitions.
A private interview between the abbot and individual monks. Each person burns incense, makes prostrations, converses, and exits.
Eight kinds of meditation for attaining liberation from mental afflictions.

The perfection (paramita) of six qualities practiced by the bodhisattva on the way to complete enlightenment: (1) giving or generosity (dana), (2) morality or keeping the precepts (sila), (3) forbearance or patience (ksanti), (4) effort (virya), (5) meditation (dhyana), and (6) wisdom (prajna).
The officer responsible for preparing meals. Burns incense and makes prostrations before gruel and the midday meal. Central figure in Dōgen's Tenzō Kyōkun (T2584).
Scheduled on the 4th, 9th, 14th, 19th, 24th, and last day of each month.
An aeon.
Third of the three bodies. 'Hundred-billion Transformation-Body Śākyamuni Buddha.'
A standard liturgical recitation: Vairocana, Rocana, Śākyamuni, Maitreya, etc.
A standing bow with hands pressed together or folded.
Four virtuous states of mind, regarded as immeasurable because they focus on all beings and can produce infinite merits: (1) loving-kindness, (2) compassion, (3) sympathetic joy, and (4) equanimity.

(1) Life is suffering; (2) our attachments are the cause of suffering; (3) all suffering can be ended; (4) the way to end suffering is by following the Buddha's eightfold right path (i.e., right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration).
The practice of directing accumulated merit to specific recipients. Also rendered 'transfer of merit.'
Karmic causation.
The hall enshrining the local earth deity.
A cloth spread for prostrations.
The central practice of Zen monasticism. May also be left untranslated as 'zazen' in Japanese context.
The cushion for seated meditation.
Officer responsible for managing a particular hall.
The officer who manages the ordination-seniority board, bed assignments, and administrative records for the Sangha hall.
Second of the three bodies. 'Complete Reward-Body Rocana Buddha.'
The dhāraṇī of Avalokiteśvara, chanted during the summer retreat and funeral rites.
Struck for three long rounds during breakfast and noon meal proceedings.
The full community of monks in residence. Ichimura renders as 'practitioners' throughout.
Lit., "son (putra) of a god (deva)," but often used synonymously with deva (god).
The large drum before the storehouse, struck in three rounds. Signals the Dharma hall ascent, entering the room, and night watches.
The ninety-day intensive training period, following the ancient Indian rain-retreat custom. Summer retreat runs from the 15th of the 4th month to the 15th of the 7th month.
The officer in charge of a particular residential quarter.
Residential rooms for monks. Various quarters exist: assembly quarters, eastern corridor, western corridor, head seat's quarters, etc.
An informal teaching session, often held in the evening. Concludes with 'The night is deep; take care of yourselves.'
Small sounding blocks kept at each quarter. Struck to signal various events.
The ritual of lowering and spreading eating bowls at meal time.
The main entrance to the monastery. Also refers to the monastery itself by metonymy.
The abbot's or head seat's formal inspection walk through the Sangha hall, burning incense.
The bimonthly precept-recitation ceremony on the 15th and last day of the month. Includes purifying water, fragrant water, tally distribution, and recitation of the bodhisattva precepts.
The administrative officer in charge of the storehouse (kitchen and provisioning).
The monastery's kitchen and provisions area. The cloud-board and drum before the storehouse signal meal times.
A small bell struck during recitations and the poṣadha tally distribution.
The future Buddha, currently still a bodhisattva.
A body of monastic rules.
Formal recitation of the Ten Buddha Names and associated dedications.
The ceremony on the 8th of the 12th month commemorating the Buddha's awakening.
Moral discipline; the precepts.
The abbot's ceremonial whisk, symbol of authority. Used during Dharma hall ascents.
The formal process by which a monk is admitted to the monastery's assembly. Includes submission of documents, bed assignment, and congratulatory prostrations.
The signal (three bell strikes) that the assembly is dismissed from formal activities.
A monk newly arrived at the monastery seeking to join the assembly, who must go through the hanging-bag registration process.
Lit. 'ten-foot square,' after Vimalakīrti's room. Refers both to the abbot's residence and, by metonymy, to the abbot himself. Also called 'sleeping hall' (寝堂, shindō).
A ritual offering of food to hungry ghosts, performed at Ullambana and on New Year's Eve. Includes mantras, mudrās, and food consecration.
An informal Dharma talk delivered to the assembly standing in a circle.
An officer among the senior ranks.
A wooden percussion instrument struck in three rounds to signal meals. Tradition holds the fish is always awake, hence its form.
A retired abbot of the home temple.
The source of the bodhisattva precepts recited during poṣadha.
Brahma conduct; the religious life; celibacy.
A major liturgical observance during the summer retreat: daily chanting of the Śūraṅgama Dhāraṇī for one hundred days.
The primary liturgical source. Its dhāraṇī is chanted daily.
Action that results in recompense or retribution; willed action; moral action.
The wooden mallet struck by the rector to signal liturgical actions. In T2025 Ch. IX, paired with the octagonal wooden post.
Struck when the abbot comes to offer incense at the Buddha hall.
A fully ordained Buddhist monk. Standard self-designation in memorials: 'heir-bhikṣu so-and-so.'
A novice monk who has taken the initial precepts but has not yet received full ordination.
An individually existing thing.

english: Record of the Duration of the Dharma
notes: Source text for the names and abodes of the sixteen arhats.
The hall where the abbot delivers formal Dharma talks. Contains the Dharma seat and drum.
The totality of reality.
Chanted during sutra-reading campaigns and the Ullambana offering.
A certain school of Buddhism.
First of the three bodies. 'Pure Dharma-Body Vairocana Buddha.'
The officer responsible for managing the monastery's bathing schedule and facilities.
Used both for the Buddha's parinirvāṇa and for the death of eminent monks.
The ceremony on the 15th of the 2nd month commemorating the Buddha's passing into parinirvāṇa.
Chanted alongside the Great Compassion Dhāraṇī.
Monastic regulations. Lit. 'pure regulations.' The genre of Chan/Zen monastic codes originating with Baizhang. Used in all text titles in this volume.
The ceremony on the 8th of the 4th month celebrating the Buddha's birth. Includes bathing the Nativity Buddha image.
Central theme. The impermanence verse: 'The day is already past; life diminishes accordingly.'
A hall used for tea services, administrative meetings, and other gatherings.
A metaphor for great eloquence in teaching the Dharma.
A formal prayer document read aloud during major ceremonies. Written on yellow paper and sealed with the Three Jewels seal.
The mid-summer festival for the salvation of ancestors, connected to Maudgalyāyana's rescue of his mother.
Describes Maudgalyāyana's offering to save his mother.
A senior administrative officer. May substitute for the abbot in burning incense at various halls.
An administrative officer who may substitute for the abbot in burning incense.
Ultimate reality. Also rendered 'suchness.'
The group of officers responsible for administrative affairs: comptroller, superintendent, guest prefect, storehouse officer, prior, bath master. Stand on the east side.
The officer who manages guest monks, new arrivals, and hanging-bag registration. Leads donors during Dharma hall ascents.
Responsible for the Buddha offering in the Sangha hall and for preparing the Nativity Buddha image on the Buddha's Birthday. Also called 'hall master' (殿主).
Used during morning and evening incense offering at the Buddha hall.
The hall enshrining memorial tablets of the ancestral patriarchs.
Meditation; the school. Use 'Chan' for Chinese context (T2025), 'Zen' for Japanese context (T2577-T2607). Leave untranslated when referring to the school.

The quality that all things have of being devoid of independent, real existence. Emptiness is emptiness of svabhava, which is independent being, autonomy, self-nature, etc. The central teaching of Mahayana Buddhism.
The formal beginning of the summer or winter training period.
The officer who manages liturgical affairs: striking the mallet, chanting dedications, reading memorials, leading recitations, and supervising the ordination-seniority board.
One who has attained liberation. The sixteen great arhats are the subject of a special offering ceremony.
A ceremony of offering to the sixteen arhats, including a five-part Dharma lecture.
The image (typically Mañjuśrī) enshrined at the center of the Sangha hall. Offerings are made at each meal.
The attendant responsible for serving the Holy Monk's shrine in the Sangha hall: managing incense during meditation, lowering lanterns, and reporting to the head seat.
Transcendent wisdom. 'Mahā-prajñā-pāramitā' concludes every set of the Ten Buddha Names.
Chanted during summer prayer services.
The supreme awakening. Merit is dedicated to 'the unsurpassed Buddha-fruit of bodhi.'
The aspiration for supreme enlightenment.
The tree under which the Buddha attained awakening in meditation.
A being who aspires to buddhahood for the sake of all sentient beings.
The officer responsible for the monastery's library and scripture collection.
The order of precedence based on the date of ordination.
The communal living quarters for the general assembly. Site of quarterly sutra chanting and reading of regulations.
A layperson training at the monastery who has not yet been ordained. Performs service roles: carrying incense, announcing releases, calling food.
The transfer-of-merit formula recited after sutra chanting, directing merit to specific recipients.
The formal monastic robe. Donned for all ceremonies with the robe verse.
A former abbot of another temple.
Chapter 25 of the Lotus Sūtra. Chanted on the 18th of each month.
The formal conclusion of the training period. The Dharma-year is said to 'come full circle.'
A form of prostration where the forehead touches the ground. Three touchings is a common courtesy.
A formal session where the head seat requests teaching on behalf of the assembly.
Formal chanting of sutras and dhāraṇīs, performed daily and at special occasions.
The elaborate exchange of prostrations and greetings on the first day of the year.
The Wheel-turning King; universal monarch.
A solitary Buddha; one who neither teaches nor is taught.
An alternate formal name for the Sangha hall, used in official documents.
A senior administrative officer responsible for financial and logistical management. Hosts tea services at the commencing of retreats.
A diamond and, by extension, anything hard and indestructible; a ritual implement shaped like a bolt and having one, three, or five prongs at each end.
Played during ceremonial incense offerings, circumambulations, funerals, and receptions.
The monk's eating bowl, lowered and spread at meal times.
The large temple bell (大鐘, dazhong). Struck 108 times (three cycles of thirty-six) for major occasions.
Closing the braziers at the end of the Nirvana Assembly period.
The memorial pagoda of the monastery's founding patriarch.
The ceremony on the 1st of the 10th month when braziers are opened for winter.
The signal (three rounds of drum and board) at the end of the hare hour (5-7 AM) for the assembly to rise.
A class of superhuman beings who are in constant conflict with the gods.

A collection of treatises explaining some of the contents of the Buddha's teachings. One of the three divisions (Tripitaka) of Buddhist scriptures. The other two are the Sutra (the sermons of the Buddha) and the Vinaya (rules of monastic discipline).
One who is in the last of the four stages of development.
Sacred formulas chanted in daily practice. Several appear in shingi texts: Śūraṅgama, Great Compassion, Disaster-Eliminating, Uṣṇīṣavijayā.
A board-shaped metal instrument hung before the storehouse, struck to signal meals. Also called 'fire board' (火板, kaban) when struck three times to signal meditation's end.
In Buddhist cosmology, the highest mountain rising from the center of the world, surrounded by an ocean in which the four continents that comprise the world of human beings are situated.

The group of officers responsible for training and practice: head seat, secretary, rector, library master, hall keeper, bath master. Stand on the west side. Also called 'training officials' in Ichimura.
A kind of ghost that always suffers from hunger and thirst.
The senior monk in the Sangha hall, responsible for supervising meditation and leading the assembly. Also called 'first seat.' There may be front hall (前堂) and rear hall (後堂) head seats.
Burned in all ceremonies. 'One stick of incense' is the standard offering unit.
